quiz_questions.question_text: What prefix is used for 3 atoms in covalent naming?

options.text: Di- (meaning two)
Tetra- (meaning four)
Tri- (meaning three)
Mono- (meaning one)

quiz_questions.explanation: The prefix tri- means three and is used in covalent compound naming to indicate three atoms of an element, such as in nitrogen trifluoride (NF3).

quiz_questions.question_text: What prefix means one in covalent compound naming?

options.text: Mono- (used for one atom)
Di- (used for two atoms)
Uni- (used for single atom)
Tri- (used for three atoms)

quiz_questions.explanation: Mono- is the prefix meaning one. It is typically only used on the second element in a covalent compound name, such as carbon monoxide (CO).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the correct name for CO2?

options.text: Carbon oxide compound
Carbon dioxide compound
Dicarbon oxide compound
Monocarbon oxide compound

quiz_questions.explanation: CO2 is carbon dioxide. Carbon has no prefix (mono- is dropped for the first element), and dioxide indicates two oxygen atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: The second element in a covalent compound name always ends in:

options.text: The suffix -ate
The suffix -ide
The suffix -ite
The suffix -ous

quiz_questions.explanation: In covalent compound naming, the second element always has the suffix -ide added to its root name, such as chlor-ide, ox-ide, or fluor-ide.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the correct name for N2O5?

options.text: Nitrogen oxide compound
Dinitrogen pentoxide
Nitrogen pentaoxide compound
Dinitrogen tetroxide compound

quiz_questions.explanation: N2O5 is dinitrogen pentoxide. Di- indicates two nitrogen atoms and penta- indicates five oxygen atoms, with -ide ending on oxide.

quiz_questions.question_text: What prefix is used for 4 atoms in covalent naming?

options.text: Tri- (meaning three)
Penta- (meaning five)
Di- (meaning two atoms)
Tetra- (meaning four)

quiz_questions.explanation: Tetra- is the prefix meaning four. For example, carbon tetrachloride (CCl4) has four chlorine atoms bonded to carbon.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the correct formula for dinitrogen monoxide?

options.text: NO2 (nitrogen dioxide formula)
N2O (dinitrogen monoxide formula)
NO (nitrogen monoxide formula)
N2O3 (dinitrogen trioxide formula)

quiz_questions.explanation: Dinitrogen means two nitrogen atoms and monoxide means one oxygen atom, giving the formula N2O, also known as laughing gas.

quiz_questions.question_text: When naming covalent compounds, the mono- prefix is dropped from:

options.text: The second element in the compound name
The first element in the compound name
Both elements in the compound name always
Neither element in the compound name ever

quiz_questions.explanation: The prefix mono- is never used on the first element. For example, CO is carbon monoxide (not monocarbon monoxide), but mono- is kept on the second element.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the correct name for PCl5?

options.text: Phosphorus chloride compound
Phosphorus pentachloride compound
Pentaphosphorus chloride compound
Monophosphorus pentachloride

quiz_questions.explanation: PCl5 is phosphorus pentachloride. There is one phosphorus (mono- is dropped for the first element) and five chlorines (penta- prefix).

quiz_questions.question_text: What prefix represents six atoms in covalent naming?

options.text: Penta- (meaning five atoms)
Hepta- (meaning seven atoms)
Hexa- (meaning six atoms)
Octa- (meaning eight atoms)

quiz_questions.explanation: Hexa- is the prefix meaning six. For example, sulfur hexafluoride (SF6) has six fluorine atoms bonded to one sulfur atom.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of a covalent compound in terms of naming?

options.text: A compound made of a metal and nonmetal using Roman numerals
A compound made of two nonmetals named using Greek prefixes
A compound named using the charge of the metal cation present
A compound named using only the element names with no prefix

quiz_questions.explanation: Covalent compounds are made of two nonmetals and are named using Greek numerical prefixes to indicate the number of each type of atom in the molecule.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the prefix 'di-' mean when used in covalent naming?

options.text: It indicates there is one atom of the element
It indicates there are two atoms of the element
It indicates there are three atoms of the element
It indicates there are four atoms of the element

quiz_questions.explanation: The prefix di- means two. It is used in names like carbon dioxide (CO2), which has two oxygen atoms, or dinitrogen monoxide (N2O), which has two nitrogen atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the prefix 'penta-' indicate in a covalent compound name?

options.text: The element has four atoms present
The element has five atoms present
The element has six atoms present
The element has seven atoms present

quiz_questions.explanation: Penta- means five. For example, phosphorus pentachloride (PCl5) contains five chlorine atoms. Dinitrogen pentoxide (N2O5) contains five oxygen atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the meaning of the suffix '-ide' in covalent compound naming?

options.text: It means the compound contains oxygen atoms in the formula
It is added to the root name of the second element in the compound
It means the compound is in the acid form when dissolved
It means the first element has been modified in the formula

quiz_questions.explanation: The suffix -ide is always added to the root of the second element's name in a covalent compound, such as chlor-ide, ox-ide, or sulfide.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between naming ionic compounds and naming covalent compounds?

options.text: Ionic compounds use Greek prefixes while covalent do not
Covalent compounds use Greek prefixes while ionic do not
Covalent compounds use Roman numerals while ionic use prefixes
There is no difference between naming these compound types

quiz_questions.explanation: Covalent compounds use Greek numerical prefixes (mono-, di-, tri-, etc.) to indicate atom counts, while ionic compounds use the metal name and charge (sometimes Roman numerals).

quiz_questions.question_text: Define the term 'binary covalent compound' in chemistry.

options.text: A covalent compound containing exactly two total atoms
A covalent compound made of exactly two different elements
A covalent compound containing two covalent bonds only
A covalent compound that exists in two different phases

quiz_questions.explanation: A binary covalent compound is made up of exactly two different nonmetal elements, such as CO2 (carbon and oxygen) or NF3 (nitrogen and fluorine).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the prefix 'hepta-' mean in covalent compound names?

options.text: It indicates six atoms of that element
It indicates eight atoms of that element
It indicates seven atoms of that element
It indicates nine atoms of that element

quiz_questions.explanation: Hepta- is the Greek prefix meaning seven. For example, dichlorine heptoxide (Cl2O7) contains seven oxygen atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the correct order for writing a covalent compound name?

options.text: Alphabetical order of the two element names always
The element farther left or lower on the periodic table is named first
The element with more atoms in the formula is always named first
The more electronegative element is always written and named first

quiz_questions.explanation: In covalent compound naming, the element that appears farther left or lower on the periodic table is named first, and the second element gets the -ide suffix.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the prefix 'octa-' indicate when naming covalent compounds?

options.text: It indicates six atoms of the element
It indicates seven atoms of the element
It indicates nine atoms of the element
It indicates eight atoms of the element

quiz_questions.explanation: Octa- is the Greek prefix meaning eight. It would be used if a covalent compound contained eight atoms of a particular element.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the systematic name for the compound with the formula SF6?

options.text: Sulfur fluoride compound
Hexasulfur fluoride compound
Sulfur hexafluoride compound
Monosulfur hexafluoride

quiz_questions.explanation: SF6 is sulfur hexafluoride. Mono- is dropped from sulfur (first element), and hexa- indicates six fluorine atoms with the -ide suffix.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is asked to name the compound P2O5. Which name should the student give?

options.text: Phosphorus oxide compound
Phosphorus pentoxide compound
Diphosphorus pentoxide
Pentaphosphorus dioxide

quiz_questions.explanation: P2O5 has two phosphorus atoms (di-) and five oxygen atoms (penta-), giving the name diphosphorus pentoxide. Both prefixes are needed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student writes 'monocarbon dioxide' as the name for CO2. What mistake did the student make?

options.text: The student should have used the prefix 'di-' on carbon instead
The student should not use 'mono-' on the first element in the name
The student used the wrong ending and it should be '-ite' not '-ide'
The student named the elements in the wrong order for the formula

quiz_questions.explanation: The prefix mono- is never used on the first element in a covalent compound name. The correct name is simply carbon dioxide, not monocarbon dioxide.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student has a bottle labeled 'dinitrogen tetroxide.' What is the chemical formula for this compound?

options.text: NO2 (one nitrogen, two oxygens)
N4O2 (four nitrogens, two oxygens)
N2O4 (two nitrogens, four oxygens)
N2O (two nitrogens, one oxygen)

quiz_questions.explanation: Dinitrogen means two nitrogen atoms and tetroxide means four oxygen atoms, so the formula is N2O4.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student sees the formula CCl4 on a lab bottle and needs to write a proper label. What should the student write?

options.text: Carbon chloride compound
Carbon tetrachloride compound
Monocarbon tetrachloride
Tetrachloride carbon compound

quiz_questions.explanation: CCl4 is carbon tetrachloride. Mono- is dropped from carbon (first element), and tetra- indicates four chlorine atoms with the -ide suffix.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is given the name 'nitrogen trifluoride' and asked to write its formula. Which formula is correct?

options.text: N3F (three nitrogens, one fluorine)
NF3 (one nitrogen, three fluorines)
N2F3 (two nitrogens, three fluorines)
NF (one nitrogen, one fluorine only)

quiz_questions.explanation: Nitrogen (no prefix means one) and trifluoride (tri- means three fluorine atoms) gives the formula NF3.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student names the compound SO3 as 'sulfur oxide.' What feedback should the teacher give?

options.text: The name is correct and complete as written by the student
The student must add the prefix 'tri-' to oxide to show there are three oxygens
The student should use Roman numerals after sulfur to show the charge
The student should change the ending to '-ate' instead of '-ide'

quiz_questions.explanation: Covalent compounds require Greek prefixes to show the number of each atom. SO3 should be named sulfur trioxide, with tri- indicating three oxygen atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student encounters two compounds: NO and NO2. How does the naming system help distinguish between them?

options.text: NO is named nitrogen oxide and NO2 is named nitrogen oxide two
NO is nitrogen monoxide and NO2 is nitrogen dioxide due to prefixes
They have the same name since both contain nitrogen and oxygen
NO is nitrogen(I) oxide and NO2 is nitrogen(II) oxide with Roman numerals

quiz_questions.explanation: Greek prefixes distinguish covalent compounds with the same elements but different numbers of atoms. Nitrogen monoxide (NO) has one oxygen; nitrogen dioxide (NO2) has two oxygens.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab report, a student writes the name 'disulfur decaoxide' for S2O10. A classmate says the prefix for 10 is wrong. What is the correct prefix for 10?

options.text: Nona- is the correct prefix for ten
Octa- is the correct prefix for ten
Deca- is the correct prefix for ten
Hexa- is the correct prefix for ten

quiz_questions.explanation: Deca- is the correct Greek prefix meaning ten. The name disulfur decaoxide would actually be correct if the formula were S2O10.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student needs to name the compound IF7 for a quiz. What is the correct name?

options.text: Iodine fluoride compound
Iodine heptafluoride compound
Monoiodine heptafluoride
Iodine hexafluoride compound

quiz_questions.explanation: IF7 is iodine heptafluoride. Mono- is not used on the first element (iodine), and hepta- indicates seven fluorine atoms.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is asked why CO is called carbon monoxide and not just carbon oxide. What is the best explanation?

options.text: The mono- is needed to distinguish CO from CO2 in the naming system
The mono- prefix is always required on every element in the name
Carbon oxide would mean the compound contains no oxygen atoms
The prefix indicates that the compound is ionic not covalent

quiz_questions.explanation: The prefix mono- on the second element is important to distinguish compounds. Without it, 'carbon oxide' could refer to either CO or CO2. The mono- clarifies there is only one oxygen.
